title: Design a Distributed Rate Limiter
description: Protect your APIs from being overwhelmed by too many requests. Learn Token Bucket and Sliding Window algorithms.

steps.detail: Drag the API Gateway onto the canvas. It acts as the entry point for all external traffic.
steps.hint: API Gateway is in the Networking section. Drag it onto the canvas, then draw a connection from Client → API Gateway.

explanation.what: A centralized entry point for all API requests. Handles authentication, SSL termination, and enforces rate limits before traffic hits your backend.
explanation.why: Rate limiting at the gateway level prevents malicious or accidental traffic floods from consuming backend compute. By rejecting requests early, you protect all downstream services simultaneously.
explanation.tradeoff: The gateway is a potential single point of failure. If it goes down, your entire API is unreachable — you must run it in active-active HA mode. Adds ~1ms to every request.
explanation.realWorld: AWS API Gateway handles billions of requests per day for companies like Airbnb and DoorDash. Kong Gateway is deployed by Moderna for rate limiting clinical trial API traffic.
explanation.capacity: AWS API Gateway: 10,000 RPS default per account (adjustable). Kong: 150k+ RPS on a 4-core instance. HAProxy: 200k+ RPS.

steps.instruction: Add a Rate Limiter Logic Node
steps.detail: Drag a Rate Limiter and connect it from the API Gateway. This implements Token Bucket counting.
steps.hint: Rate Limiter is in the Networking section. Drag it onto the canvas, then connect API Gateway → Rate Limiter.

explanation.what: The component that executes the actual counting algorithm — Token Bucket, Sliding Window Log, or Fixed Window Counter — to decide whether to allow or reject a request.
explanation.why: Separating rate-limiting logic from the gateway allows fine-grained policies per user tier (free: 60 req/min, pro: 1000 req/min) without redeploying the gateway itself.
explanation.tradeoff: Adds an internal network hop (~0.2ms). The algorithm choice matters: Token Bucket allows bursts, Leaky Bucket enforces constant output, Sliding Window Log is most accurate but memory-intensive.
explanation.realWorld: Stripe's payment API uses a custom rate limiter library backed by Redis. GitHub's REST API enforces 5,000 requests/hour for authenticated tokens and 60/hour for unauthenticated. Twitter's streaming API allows 1% of all global tweets per connected app.
explanation.capacity: Algorithm throughput in-process: ~1M decisions/sec. Bottleneck shifts to the Redis state store.

steps.detail: Drag a Cache (Redis) and connect it from the Rate Limiter. This stores counters across all server instances.
steps.hint: Cache (Redis) is in the Storage section. Drag it onto the canvas, then connect Rate Limiter → Cache to store distributed counters.

explanation.what: In-memory store that holds per-user/per-IP request counters with TTL expiration. Redis atomic INCR + EXPIRE operations make counter updates safe under concurrency.
explanation.why: Without a shared store, each API server tracks counters independently. A user with 3 servers can make 3x the allowed requests — one per server — defeating the rate limiter entirely.
explanation.tradeoff: Redis becomes a hot path dependency. If Redis latency spikes from 0.5ms to 10ms, every API request is delayed. Mitigate with Redis Cluster or a local leaky-bucket fallback.
explanation.realWorld: GitHub rate limits are tracked in a dedicated Redis cluster with sentinel failover. Cloudflare uses a distributed in-house key-value store across 300+ PoPs to enforce per-IP rate limits at sub-millisecond speed.
explanation.capacity: Redis single-threaded: 100k INCR/sec on 1 core. Redis Cluster: 1M+ ops/sec across 6 shards. Use EVAL scripts to atomically check-and-increment in a single RTT.

steps.detail: Drag an API Server and connect it from the Rate Limiter. Allowed requests pass through to this layer.
steps.hint: API Server is in the Compute section. Drag it onto the canvas, then connect Rate Limiter → API Server so allowed requests reach your backend.

explanation.what: The application logic layer that only receives requests the rate limiter has explicitly permitted. Returns HTTP 429 Too Many Requests back through the limiter for rejected traffic.
explanation.why: By positioning the API server behind the rate limiter, you guarantee it only processes legitimate traffic. This protects expensive operations like database writes from abuse.
explanation.tradeoff: Adding a rate limiter in-path increases request latency by 0.5-2ms (Redis round trip). For latency-sensitive APIs (<5ms SLA), consider embedding the limiter in the API server process itself.
explanation.realWorld: Twitter's API tier system serves 500k developers. Their backend API servers only process requests that passed the rate limiter. Rejected requests return in <5ms — cheaper than executing real API logic.
explanation.capacity: Node.js API server: 10k-50k RPS depending on workload. Rate limiter overhead: <1ms p99 when Redis is colocated in the same AZ.

steps.detail: Drag a Monitoring node and connect it from the API Server. Track rate limit hit rates, Redis latency, and abuse patterns.
steps.hint: Monitoring is in the Observability section. Drag it onto the canvas, then connect API Server → Monitoring.

explanation.what: Metrics pipeline that tracks: rate limit hit rate (% of requests rejected), Redis p99 latency, requests per user/IP, abuse patterns, and quota exhaustion events.
explanation.why: Without visibility, you can't tune limits. If 15% of requests are being rate-limited, either limits are too strict or you have an active abuse campaign. You need data to tell the difference.
explanation.tradeoff: Monitoring adds a small write overhead (emitting metrics). High cardinality labels (per-user metrics) can overwhelm Prometheus or Datadog — use aggregated counters plus sampling for user-level data.
explanation.realWorld: Twilio monitors rate limit breach events in real-time. Sudden spikes (>10x baseline rejections) trigger automated alerts and optionally block IPs via Cloudflare's Firewall API. They publish rate limit dashboards in their status page.
explanation.capacity: Datadog agent: <1% CPU overhead. Prometheus + Grafana: handles 1M+ time-series on a single machine. Budget for 2-5% of total request volume as monitoring overhead.

questions.question: Which algorithm is best for allowing short bursts of traffic while maintaining a steady average rate?

explanation.correct: Token Bucket accumulates tokens over time up to a bucket capacity. Unused capacity builds up, allowing a burst equal to bucket size before being rate-limited. This matches real traffic patterns where users sometimes send many requests at once.

wrongAnswers.a: Fixed Window allows a 'double burst' at window boundaries — a user can send N requests at the end of window 1 and N more at the start of window 2.
wrongAnswers.c: Leaky Bucket drains requests at a constant rate regardless of available capacity. It smooths traffic but forbids any burst whatsoever.
wrongAnswers.d: Sliding Window Log is the most accurate but stores a timestamp per request in memory — O(N) space where N is the request count in the window.

questions.question: Why must rate limiter counters be stored in Redis rather than in-process memory?

options.text: Redis is faster than in-process memory
With 3 API servers, each tracks separately — users can exceed limits by sending to different servers
In-process counters restart on every request
Redis automatically enforces rate limits without code

explanation.correct: In a horizontally scaled system, a user limited to 100 req/min can hit 300 req/min total by distributing requests across 3 servers — each counting independently. Shared Redis state ensures the global limit is honored.

wrongAnswers.a: In-process memory is orders of magnitude faster than Redis. The Redis tradeoff is consistency across instances, not raw speed.
wrongAnswers.c: In-process counters persist across requests — they only reset if the process crashes or restarts.
wrongAnswers.d: Redis provides atomic operations (INCR, EXPIRE) but requires application code to implement the rate limiting logic.

questions.question: GitHub allows 5,000 API requests per hour for authenticated users. What HTTP status code should be returned when this limit is exceeded?

explanation.correct: RFC 6585 defines HTTP 429 specifically for rate limiting. Well-designed APIs also include Retry-After and X-RateLimit-Remaining headers so clients know when to retry.

wrongAnswers.a: 400 indicates a malformed request — the syntax is wrong, not the volume.
wrongAnswers.b: 401 means the client is not authenticated at all — unrelated to request volume.
wrongAnswers.d: 503 means the server is temporarily unavailable, typically due to overload or maintenance — not a policy enforcement response.
